concepts.title: Introduction to exponential form & argand plane
concepts.conceptBody:
Euler's Formula

A useful expression is Euler's formula which expresses an exponential with imaginary argument in terms of a sum of real and imaginary parts:

${{\text{e}}^{\text{i}\theta }}=\text{cos}\theta +\text{isin}\theta $

Exponential form : Using Euler's formula, it is possible to compactly write a complex number in terms of an exponential function:

$z=x+iy\equiv r\left( \text{cos}\theta +i\text{sin}\theta \right)=r{{e}^{i\theta }}$

On an Argand diagram, complex numbers with the same modulus $r=\left| z \right|$ but different arguments $\theta $ make up points on a circle centered on the origin with radius $r=\left| z \right|$. For modulus special points of interest (for $\text{r}=1$ ) are:

\(\begin{align} & \theta =0\Rightarrow \text{z}={{\text{e}}^{\text{i}0}}=1\text{ real } \\ & \theta =\pi /2\Rightarrow \text{z}={{\text{e}}^{\text{i}\pi /2}}=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{\pi }}/2+\text{isi}{{\text{n}}^{\pi }}/2=\text{i }\text{ Imaginary} \\ & \theta =\pi \Rightarrow z={{e}^{i\pi }}=\text{cos}\pi +i\text{sin}\pi =-1\text{ real } \\ & \theta =3\pi /2\Rightarrow z={{e}^{i3\pi /2}}=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{3\pi /}}/2+\text{isi}{{\text{n}}^{3\pi /2}}=-\text{i }\text{ Imaginary } \\ & \theta =2\pi \Rightarrow z={{e}^{i2\pi }}=\text{cos}2\pi +i\text{sin}2\pi =1\text{ real } \\ \end{align}\)

Geometrical representation of Complex Numbers 

Geometrically, we can represent complex numbers on a plane (known as Argand Plane).

This plane consists of two perpendicular lines known as real axis & imaginary axis. Real axis is drawn horizontally and imaginary axis is vertical to it. Any complex number can be represented as a point on this plane.

Let $\text{z}=\text{a}+\text{bi}$ be the point to be represented on this plane. This point is represented through the order pair ( $a,b)$ on the plane. Real part (a) is plotted along the real axis and imaginary part (b) is plotted along the imaginary axis.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{a}=\text{Re}\left( \text{z} \right)=\text{ horizontal }\text{ component } \\ {} & \text{b}=\text{Im}\left( \text{z} \right)=\text{ vertical }\text{ component } \\ \end{array}$

The Argand diagram 

In two dimensional Cartesian coordinates $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$, we are used to plotting the function $\text{y}\left( \text{x} \right)$ with $\text{y}$ on the vertical axis and $\text{x}$ on the horizontal axis.

In an Argand diagram, the complex number $\text{z}=\text{x}+$ iy is plotted as a single point with coordinates $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$. The horizontal axis is called the real axis (x-axis) and the vertical axis is called the imaginary axis (y-axis).

As in usual Cartesian coordinates, the distance from the origin to a point $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ is equal to $\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}}$. This is equal to the modulus $\left| \text{z} \right|$ of the complex number $\text{z}=\text{x}+\text{iy}$.

The Argand diagram may also be called the complex plane. It stresses that complex numbers are a generalization of real numbers, that lie on the horizontal axis only.

The expression $\text{z}=\text{x}+$ iy is known as the Cartesian form or the rectangular form of the complex number z. Using the Argand diagram, we can see that the addition of complex numbers behaves like the addition of vectors.

If we express $\text{z}=\text{x}+$ iy as an ordered pair $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$, then the addition of two complex numbers may be defined by $\left( x,y \right)+\left( a,b \right)=\left( x+a,y+b \right)$ in the same way as the addition of two vectors.

concepts.title: Representation of complex number in polar coordinates
concepts.conceptBody:
Polar representation of complex numbers 

A position vector of a point in two dimensions may be expressed in terms of Cartesian coordinates $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ and plotted with $\text{y}$ on the vertical axis and $\text{x}$ on the horizontal axis.

It is also possible to express the two dimensional position vector in terms of polar coordinates ($r$, $\theta $) where $r$ is the magnitude of the vector (distance from origin to the point) and $\theta $ is the angle between the position vector and the positive $\text{x}$-axis.

The Cartesian and polar coordinates are related by:

\(\begin{array}{r}x=r \cos \theta, y=r \sin \theta \\ r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}, \tan \theta=\frac{y}{x}\end{array}\)

In the same way, the complex number $z=x+$ iy may be expressed in polar coordinates $\left( r,\theta \right)$ in its polar form:

$\text{z}=\text{x}+\text{iy}\equiv \text{r}\left( \text{cos}\theta +\text{isin}\theta \right)$

 

where $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }r=\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}},\text{tan}\theta =\frac{y}{x}$

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{z}=1+\sqrt{3\text{i}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=1,\text{y}=\sqrt{3} \\ {} & \text{r}=\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}}=\sqrt{4}=2 \\ {} & \text{tan}\theta =\sqrt{3}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\pi /3\left( 60 \right) \\ \end{array}$
